author: @CarstenHodes
text:
Nice video. I tried to build a ventilation tower myself some years ago, i used three 20cm Pc-fans for this, stacked on top, vertically. But there is a main component of the fan you compared your build one to: the grill. Yours has none, the one you bought has.

At the time i was building mine, i noticed quite a difference in sound and noiselevel for the different types of mesh i tried to use as a fancover. I think, the type of cover is more important for the quality and loudness of noise, even more as the shape of the blades.

If your fan doesn't need one (because its safe to touch), it's perfect. But that was something not pointed out in the video, so maybe it's possible to get an update for that. Or some test of different covers, if needed to keep some fingers safe.

Greetings from Oldenburg, Germany ;-)

replies.author: @vexy1987
replies.text: Yes, I used similar blade design to 'upgrade' my traditional mains powered fans and I noticed a huge difference before and after mounting a shroud/cover. Certainly the feeling of the wind tunel more than anything!

author: @PhilR0gers
text:
Most internal PC fans are 12v.  To run off USB, the fan needs to be 5v - look for a fan that already has a USB connection and you know it will work ok.
Might also want to print a grille for the front if there's any danger of people sticking their fingers in.

author: @stupidscruff
text:
I bought a large old air conditioner non working for £50 a couple of months ago, found the start capacitor for the exhaust fan was faulty which was why it was overheating and replaced it, full clean out when I stripped it down and checked the pipes etc, all good, works like new. 
Got foil lined air ducts, outdoor vents and am now sizing up a panel to go in a window frame for the air in and out cycle vents, the machine will bring house temperature down to a comfortable level running for about 20mins, works our about half a kw.
Haven't needed the dryer which uses at least 2kw thanks to the weather so technically on paper it will only cost me just under £100 and a few quid here and there to have an air conditioning in the house for summer, makes sense to me! 😂
I'm pretty strapped myself so after following the weather scientists and the el nina stuff started getting bit by bit so there was no big outlay of cash.

author: @elvendragonhammer5433
text: Like the idea & especially the price, here's a idea for repurposing If you have a Noctua NF-A12x25 (The one that you can see the metal hub in the center, you could just pull the entire blade shell assembly off, by putting pressure on the metal hub while pulling @ the blade housing & scale up the design for it- then you could just slide it down over that metal hub & viola' a fast fan that should run for years with no more fuss, & if you ever needed it again you could always swap the original blade shell back on it.

author: @krasslofw.4393

author: @MisterkeTube
text: Great video - as always.  But am I the only one who finds it scandalous that something that was already patented so long ago can still be patented again, blocking people from freely using things like this - unless you're willing to spent $$$ on lawyers to defend against patent claims ...  The patent system is just rotten!

replies.author: @vexy1987
replies.text: Its not blocking me!

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: You can simply ignore the patent if you know that prior art has already existed over 25 years ago . Used to be 17 years. Robert for example showed us prior state of art  ;  it's sufficient to hold MIT at bay from taking action . I recall Robert once told us that MIT is known to take out patents for prior expired inventions .

replies.author: @partciudgam8478
replies.text:
You can patent the "novel idea of usage" aka "I pattented the use of a paperclip to remove dried snot out of the nose", vs "I patented the paperclip for holding documents again", so, you can use the toroidal propeller to make a noiseless fan, and the next guy can patent it to make a quiet fume extractor, or you just alter the proportions of the propeller (changing the geometry) to give it the novel use "yes, you pattented the noiseless fan, but my modification allous for easier production, and the design is different, so I owe you no royalties".
an industrial microbiology teacher taught us that nowdays you don't want to patent a modified e. Coli that produces insulin, because that leaves the door open for someone to take a yeast and your process and make a new patent, you patent the whole process to get some form of protection for your invention.

replies.author: @woodturnersteve
replies.text: I agree re-patenting something is wrong.  However, I have been told by legal counsel that I can make anything with a patent as long as I don’t make money on it. That is where companies will go after people.

replies.author: @d.marbus1493
replies.text: There are loads of age old patents being repatented. Just tweek it enough or find another use not mentioned in the old (or repatented) patent. Clay pot  water coolers and food lockers with a fan,  Bingo
